tagged_story_for_tts:
Silent metal fatigue.<[silence:40ms]> Invisible cracks grow.<[silence:40ms]>

The term stress corrosion cracking describes how metals, under constant tension, slowly fracture when exposed to corrosive environments—like a rubber band snapping after years of being stretched in saltwater.<[silence:40ms]>

Metals appear strong, but microscopic flaws act like hidden notches.<[silence:40ms]> Add stress (a bridge’s weight, a pipeline’s pressure) and a corrosive agent (salt spray, acidic rain), and atoms at the flaw’s edge dissolve faster than others.<[silence:40ms]> The crack advances—not from brute force, but from a chemical whisper.<[silence:40ms]> A stainless steel pipe might hold for decades, then split in hours once the crack reaches critical length.<[silence:40ms]>

Real-world failures reveal its stealth: In 1965, a F-111 fighter jet’s wing snapped mid-flight due to moisture seeping into tiny stresses.<[silence:40ms]> In 2013, a Florida sinkhole swallowed a man when underground pipes, weakened by sulfuric acid in soil, collapsed.<[silence:40ms]> Engineers fight it with sacrificial coatings (like zinc on bolts) or alloys that "heal" micro-cracks—but no metal is immune forever.<[silence:40ms]>

Three related subjects are metal memory effects, hydrogen embrittlement, and how bridges secretly age.<[silence:40ms]>
clean_story:
Silent metal fatigue. Invisible cracks grow.

The term stress corrosion cracking describes how metals, under constant tension, slowly fracture when exposed to corrosive environments—like a rubber band snapping after years of being stretched in saltwater.

Metals appear strong, but microscopic flaws act like hidden notches. Add stress (a bridge’s weight, a pipeline’s pressure) and a corrosive agent (salt spray, acidic rain), and atoms at the flaw’s edge dissolve faster than others. The crack advances—not from brute force, but from a chemical whisper. A stainless steel pipe might hold for decades, then split in hours once the crack reaches critical length.

Real-world failures reveal its stealth: In 1965, a F-111 fighter jet’s wing snapped mid-flight due to moisture seeping into tiny stresses. In 2013, a Florida sinkhole swallowed a man when underground pipes, weakened by sulfuric acid in soil, collapsed. Engineers fight it with sacrificial coatings (like zinc on bolts) or alloys that "heal" micro-cracks—but no metal is immune forever.

Three related subjects are metal memory effects, hydrogen embrittlement, and how bridges secretly age.
